content.text:
# Quality Pass — Findings Report

**Scope:** 8 lenses, full functional run + full content sweep. **Disposition:** catalog only, nothing fixed. Branch `main`, tree clean (I cleaned the one stray artifact my own test run produced).

## Headline

The product is in **strong shape**. Everything *mechanical and functional* is green: the strict gate passes, all six mutating labs fail-clean-and-pass-on-solution, the read-only injection lab's fence holds, both substrates are healthy, the version system is honest, and there are **no correctness, accessibility, or authenticity defects**. Nothing here is release-blocking.

The findings concentrate in **one area you correctly anticipated**: the *ergonomics of learner-facing prose*. And your two instincts turn out to share a single root cause.

---

## What I verified clean (so you know the coverage)

| Lens | Result |
|---|---|
| **1 · Lab integrity** | ✅ All 7 verifiers behave: 6 mutating labs red-on-`main` (right-reason) / green-on-`solution/`; u03 zero-side-effects passes. u09 behavior-equivalence (35-cmd battery) + split gate both fire. |
| **2 · Substrate** | ✅ taskflow-api 36 tests green (1,145 LOC); legacy is *authentically* messy; `make check` + `doctor` green (real `claude -p` round-trip; CLI 2.1.158 matches record). |
| **3 · Accuracy/version** | ✅ Exactly 2 unverified keys (`ci`, `managed-settings`) — matches L1; no hardcoded version literals bypass `{{vd:}}`. |
| **5 · Consistency** | ✅ All 16 units identical 8-section structure; all `depth_tier: core` (uniform template is correct, not a tier violation). |
| **7 · Authenticity** | ✅ Every R14 dogfood artifact exists (`close-unit`, `prime-context`, `check-on-edit` hook, MCP fixtures); baseline `CLAUDE.md` accurate. |
| **8 · Accessibility** | ✅ Emoji only ever verbatim CLI output (`✓ Connected`, `⏸ Pending approval`) or text-labeled (`⚠️ UNTRUSTED INPUT`) — no meaning-by-emoji-alone. |

---

## Findings (severity-ranked)

Severity is relative to a *finished, working* product — read these as polish, not breakage. None block release.

### 🟠 M1 — Spec requirement-IDs (`R#`/`R#.AC#`) leak into learner prose
**~30 instances across ~7 units** (U1, U10, U11, U12, U13, U14, U16). Examples:
- U1:84 — *"an **authentic dogfooding artifact** (R14)"*
- U12:167 / U10:166 — *"Self-check (objective checklist — R7.AC3):"*
- U14:129/136 — *"The standard lives in one place (R13)"*

These are **traceability breadcrumbs** from the spec layer. There is **no learner-facing key for `R#` anywhere in `course/`** (requirement IDs live only in `requirements.md`). A learner reading "(R7.AC3)" can't decode it and has no reason to. Traceability is *already* enforced by front-matter `requirements:` + the coverage/traceability checks — so these inline cites are redundant even for maintenance.
*(One legitimate exception: U10:69 uses `R1`/`R2.AC3` to **teach** what requirement IDs look like — keep that.)*
**Direction:** strip `R#` parentheticals from learner prose (or demote to HTML comments). This is the single most defensible, lowest-risk cleanup.

### 🟠 M2 — Pervasive bare taxonomy-code cross-refs (`U#`, `C#`, `W#`, `CV`)
This is the one you named ("R1 vs U1 vs §1"). Unit bodies cite codes bare and assume internalization: *"advances `C1`"*, *"that is `U3`"*, *"what `U16` automates"*. Often **redundant** with adjacent plain language — e.g. U5:47: *"land a change (U1), read unfamiliar code (U2), operate safely (U3)…"* where the prose already says what each unit is.

These codes *are* keyed (glossary defines `W#`/`C#`/`CV`; the checklist defines `C#` with home units) — so they're decodable, unlike `R#`. But the bare-code habit is what creates the **same root cause behind both your concerns**: it (a) forces the "which letter means what" decode, and (b) the parentheticals chop sentence flow, producing the **terse/clipped texture** you felt. Fixing this addresses terseness *and* ID-confusion at once.
**Direction:** prefer human phrasing — "in **Operate safely (Unit 3)**" over "(U3)"; drop codes that merely echo adjacent prose. Keep codes in front matter, checklist, and rubric (where they're load-bearing and defined).

### 🟡 L1 — Concept-section density/altitude
The most compressed passages (Unit 1's *Concept* especially) read like **reference/spec prose** — bold-fragment lead-ins, em-dashes, semicolon-chained clauses — rather than teaching prose that breathes. Note this is **uneven**: Unit 5's Concept is genuinely good narrative teaching. So it's not systemic terseness; it's specific dense passages (and M2's parentheticals amplify the feel).
**Direction:** a light editorial pass on the densest Concept sections; let key ideas land in their own sentence.

### 🟡 L2 — `reading_time_min` under-states the heavier units
Autonomy units run 30–50% over their declared time at 200 wpm: **U13 declares 8 min / ~12 actual**, U14 8/~11, U12 8/~10. For dense technical prose the real gap is larger. Erodes trust in the estimates.
**Direction:** recompute from word count (and these are *reading* only — lab time is separate).

### ⚪ L3 — "CV" introduced unexpanded in units
Units use *"(CV)"* and *"Verify (CV):"* as if known; it's defined in the glossary/checklist as cross-cutting verification but never expanded on first use in a unit. Minor jargon-on-first-contact.
**Direction:** expand once per unit on first use, or rely on the spelled-out "verification is the through-line."

### ⚪ Known / not new — L1 version debt
`ci` + `managed-settings` remain `unverified` — already tracked in the open-loops ledger, blocked by environment access (no GitHub Actions / enterprise account). Confirmed accurate, not a regression.

---

## Read of it

Your instincts were right and **they converge**: the dominant issue is a *code-citation reflex* (M1 + M2) — the build pulled spec/traceability notation into learner-facing prose. It's the same habit that makes IDs confusing *and* makes the prose feel clipped. The good news is it's **mechanical to fix and low-risk** (deleting/rephrasing parentheticals doesn't touch labs, checks, or traceability, since those live in front matter). L1–L3 are lighter editorial polish on top.

Notably, **none of this is structural** — the architecture, labs, substrates, and enforcement are sound. This is a finishing pass on voice, not a rebuild.

---

## Next step — your call

Since you asked me to fix nothing, here's where I'd hand back to you:

Good — I'll take that as: do the work, but because M2 (rephrasing cross-refs) and L1 (density) are **voice** changes across all 16 units, doing them blind in one shot risks getting the voice wrong 16 times. So the disciplined path is: **(1)** a persistent checklist that survives session boundaries, **(2)** a full pilot on Unit 1 so you can approve the voice, then **(3)** roll it across the rest. I'll pause after the pilot for your sign-off on the voice — that's the one genuine judgment fork.

Let me set up the spanning checklist and do the U1 pilot.

# Quality-pass remediation checklist (2026-05-30)

**Origin:** post-v1 systematic quality pass (8 lenses; functional + content). Mechanical gate, lab
integrity, substrates, version system, authenticity, accessibility all verified **clean** — findings
are confined to learner-facing prose ergonomics. **This is polish, not breakage; nothing here blocks
release.** This file is the spanning tracker so the work survives session boundaries (continuity
hygiene). Tracked as open-loop **L8** in `decisions.md`.

## Findings → remediation

- **M1 — strip spec requirement-IDs (`R#`/`R#.AC#`) from learner prose.** No learner-facing key
  exists for `R#`; traceability already lives in unit front-matter `requirements:` + the checks.
  **Keep the one teaching exception** (U10 `Concept` uses `R1`/`R2.AC3` to *illustrate* what
  requirement IDs look like). Demote any genuinely useful maintainer cite to an HTML comment.
- **M2 — rephrase bare taxonomy codes (`U#`, `C#`, `W#`, `CV`) into human language.** Prefer
  title-first ("**Operate safely**" not "`U3`"); drop codes that merely echo adjacent prose; keep
  codes only where load-bearing + defined (front matter, progress-checklist, rubric, glossary).
- **L1 — relieve density in the most compressed `Concept` passages.** Let key ideas land in their
  own sentence; lighten bold-fragment/em-dash/semicolon pile-ups. Light touch — do **not** bloat
  reading time. (Prose is generally good; this targets specific dense spots, e.g. U1.)
- **L2 — recompute `reading_time_min`.** Under-declared on heavier units (U13 8→~12, U14 8→~11,
  U12 8→~10). Reading time only (lab time is separate). Mechanical.
- **L3 — expand `CV` on first use** per unit (cross-cutting verification), then the code is fine.

## Per-unit grid

Legend: `R#` = has requirement-ID leak (M1) · all units need M2/L3 sweep · `rt` = reading_time fix (L2).

| Unit | M1 (R#) | M2 (codes) | L1 (density) | L2 (rt) | L3 (CV) | done |
|------|---------|-----------|--------------|---------|---------|------|
| U1 onboarding        | yes (R14) | yes | yes (Concept) | ok (8/~6) | yes | ⬜ PILOT |
| U2 explore           | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U3 operate-safely    | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U4 memory-context    | yes (R14×2) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U5 ship-feature      | — | yes | ok (good prose) | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U6 tdd               | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U7 debug             | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U8 git-pr            | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U9 legacy-refactor   | yes (R7.AC3) | yes | review | ok (11/~12) | yes | ⬜ |
| U10 spec-driven      | yes (keep teaching R1/R2.AC3; strip R3.AC2/R7.AC3) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U11 review           | yes (R7.AC3) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U12 commands-skills  | yes (R14, R7.AC3×2) | yes | review | **rt 8→10** | yes | ⬜ |
| U13 subagents        | yes (R7.AC3×2) | yes | review | **rt 8→12** | yes | ⬜ |
| U14 hooks            | yes (R14, R13×2) | yes | review | **rt 8→11** | yes | ⬜ |
| U15 mcp-vetting      | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U16 automate-scale   | yes (R14, R12, R15) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |

**Cross-cutting files to sweep after units:** `course/capstone/{README,briefs,rubric,case-study}.md`,
`course/stuck.md`, `course/maintainer-guide.md` (maintainer-facing — `R#`/codes may be *appropriate*
here; sweep with judgment, don't strip load-bearing rubric `[Cn]` tags).

**Verification after each batch:** `make check` (links/refs/traceability must stay green — none of
these edits touch front-matter `requirements:`/`can_do:` or the rubric `[Cn]` tags, so traceability
is unaffected by design).

## Status

- [ ] U1 pilot complete → **PAUSE for voice approval** (current step)
- [ ] U2–U16 rolled after approval
- [ ] Cross-cutting files swept
- [ ] `make check` green; reading times recomputed
- [ ] L8 struck through in `decisions.md`; IMPLEMENTATION §3 noted

---
id: U1
title: "Get set up and land your first verified win"
stage: first-wins
depth_tier: core
use_case: "Get set up and make a first verified win against taskflow-api"
can_do: [C1, C2]

coverage_areas: [1, 27]
prerequisites: []

lab_time_min: 15

# Get set up and land your first verified win

## Learning objectives

By the end of this unit you can:

- **Install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code setup with a single repeatable
  command** (the `doctor` preflight) — advances `C1`.
- **Explain the agentic loop in one breath and drive a single, verified one-line change into a
  real codebase** — advances `C2`.

## Fast path (TL;DR)

> Run [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor). When it is green, open an interactive session in
> `codebases/primary/taskflow-api/`, ask Claude to add a `service` field to the `/health`
> response, **read the one-line diff before accepting it**, then run
> `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. Green check = first win. That is the whole unit.

## Skip-check

**Skip this unit if you can already:** install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code
setup with a repeatable check; and explain what the agentic loop is while landing a single,
*verified* one-line change against a real codebase (verified by you reading the diff and a check
passing — not by trusting that Claude said it was done).

## Concept

You are an experienced engineer; this unit does not teach the terminal or Git. It teaches the two
things that are genuinely new when you add Claude Code to your workflow: **how the tool operates**
and **why verification is now your job, explicitly.**

**The agentic loop.** Claude Code is not autocomplete. It runs a loop: it **observes** (reads
files, runs commands, inspects output), **acts** (proposes an edit, runs a command), and then the
**result feeds back in** — and it repeats until the goal is met or it hands back to you. Two
consequences follow, and the rest of the course builds on them:

- It is **tool-using and stateful.** It can read your repo and run commands, so what it does
  depends on what it can see and what you let it do (permissions — that is `U3`).
- It is **non-deterministic.** Ask twice, get two differently-worded answers, sometimes two
  different approaches. So you never verify *wording*; you verify *behavior and the diff.*

**Verification is the through-line (CV).** The single habit that separates an elite operator from
someone who got burned by AI is this: **green tests are necessary, not sufficient — read the
diff.** A passing suite tells you nothing broke that was already tested; it does not tell you the
change is the one you wanted, or that it didn't quietly touch something else. Every lab in this
course ends with an explicit verification step. This unit's is the smallest possible version of
it, on purpose: one line, one diff, one check.

**The REPL.** {{vd:cli-basics}} You will live in the interactive session for daily work; the
non-interactive (`-p`) form is what `doctor` uses under the hood and what `U16` automates.

**Your baseline configuration (established here, taught later).** This unit drops a known-good
starter [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md) and a minimal
`.claude/settings.json` into the practice codebase so every later lab starts from the same place.
We are only *establishing* the baseline now — what project memory and `{{vd:settings}}` /
`{{vd:memory}}` actually do, and how to shape them, is the job of `U4` (Memory & context). Don't
edit them yet.

**Awareness — IDE integration.** Claude Code also runs inside your editor: {{vd:ide}} The course
labs are written for the terminal so they're portable, but the IDE path is fully supported; reach
for it if that's where you live. (We don't drill it.)

**Version currency.** This content was verified against Claude Code `{{vd:_verified_version}}`. If
your installed version differs, `doctor` will say so and point you at
[`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md), where any version-specific
differences are documented.

## Worked example

The preflight is itself an **authentic dogfooding artifact** (R14): [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor)
is the very script this course's maintainers run, and it is also a worked example of *scripting
Claude Code* — its auth check drives the CLI headlessly and inspects the result, the same pattern
`U16` generalizes. It reports pass/fail for four things, in order:

1. **install** — `claude` is on your `PATH`.
2. **version** — `claude --version` parses, and is compared to the recorded verified version.
3. **auth + first command** — it runs one real non-interactive command and checks it round-trips,
   which proves authentication actually works (not just that a token exists).
4. **tooling deps** — the course's own check suite can run.

Run it:

```bash
tools/doctor              # all checks (step 3 makes one real call)
tools/doctor --no-probe   # skip the network round-trip (offline / CI)
tools/doctor --manual     # also print the manual checklist fallback
```

If any check can't run, the same script prints a **manual checklist** so you are never blocked by
the tool meant to unblock you.

## Lab

**Goal:** land your first verified change — have Claude add a `service` field to the `/health`
endpoint of `taskflow-api`, so it returns `{"status": "ok", "service": "taskflow-api"}` — and
prove it, by reading the diff and running the check.

**Starting state:** `start/u01-lab1` (run `tools/reset-lab u01-lab1` to restore it). It is the
clean, green primary codebase plus your baseline config — no bug is planted; the "win" is the
addition.

**Steps:**

1. Run `tools/doctor`. Do not start the lab until it is green (or you've walked the manual
   checklist). An auth wall mid-task is the most common way a first session stalls.
2. Start an interactive session from inside the codebase:
   `cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api && claude`.
3. Orient first (this is the loop's "observe"): ask Claude *"What does the `/health` endpoint
   return today, and which file defines it?"* Confirm its answer against the file yourself.
4. Make the change: ask Claude to *"add a `service` field set to `taskflow-api` to the `/health`
   response, as a one-line change."*
5. **Read the diff before you accept it** (see *Verify* below). Then accept and commit it.

**Self-check (objective):** run `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. It passes only if the full pytest
suite is still green **and** `GET /health` now returns `"service": "taskflow-api"` alongside
`"status": "ok"`.

**Reference solution:** branch `solution/u01-lab1` — attempt the lab unaided first, then diff your
result against it.

**Verify (CV):** before accepting, read the proposed diff and confirm three things with your own
eyes: (a) it touches **only** the `/health` return line, (b) it *adds* the `service` field without
dropping the existing `"status": "ok"`, and (c) nothing else in the file moved. Then run the
self-check. This 30-second habit — not the passing check — is the actual skill this unit teaches.

> **Safety note.** This change is deliberately tiny and fully reversible (`git restore` /
> `git checkout` undoes it). Reviewing the diff *before* accepting is the safe-by-default path you
> will use for every change from here on, including the large ones where it matters far more.

## Common pitfalls

- **Accepting the edit without reading the diff.** The entire point of a deliberately trivial
  first task is to build the verify-the-diff reflex while the stakes are zero. Don't skip it here;
  you'll regret skipping it on a 200-line change later.
- **Skipping `doctor` and hitting an auth wall mid-lab.** Run the preflight first; a failed auth
  three steps into a task is disorienting and easy to misread as "Claude broke."
- **Expecting deterministic output.** Claude may phrase the change or its explanation differently
  than this page or than your last run. That's normal — verify the *resulting behavior and diff*,
  never the wording.
- **Treating the baseline config as homework.** The shipped `CLAUDE.md` / `settings.json` are
  established for you on purpose. Resist tuning them now; `U4` is where that pays off.

## Going deeper

- **Next:** U2 (Explore a codebase) puts Claude to work understanding code you've never seen; U3
  (Operate safely) makes the permission/blast-radius model explicit; U4 (Memory & context)
  explains the baseline config you just established.
- [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor) — read the script; it's short, and it's your model for
  the headless pattern in U16.
- [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) — where version drift is recorded if
  your CLI differs from the verified version.
- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).
</content>
</invoke>

# Get set up and land your first verified win

## Learning objectives

By the end of this unit you can:

- **Install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code setup with a single repeatable
  command** (the `doctor` preflight).
- **Explain the agentic loop in one breath and drive a single, verified one-line change into a
  real codebase.**

## Fast path (TL;DR)

> Run [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor). When it is green, open an interactive session in
> `codebases/primary/taskflow-api/`, ask Claude to add a `service` field to the `/health`
> response, **read the one-line diff before accepting it**, then run
> `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. Green check = first win. That is the whole unit.

## Skip-check

**Skip this unit if you can already:** install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code
setup with a repeatable check; and explain what the agentic loop is while landing a single,
*verified* one-line change against a real codebase (verified by you reading the diff and a check
passing — not by trusting that Claude said it was done).

## Concept

You are an experienced engineer; this unit does not teach the terminal or Git. It teaches the two
things that are genuinely new when you add Claude Code to your workflow: **how the tool operates**
and **why verification is now your job, explicitly.**

**The agentic loop.** Claude Code is not autocomplete. It runs a loop: it **observes** (reads
files, runs commands, inspects output), **acts** (proposes an edit, runs a command), and then the
**result feeds back in** — and it repeats until the goal is met or it hands back to you. Two
consequences follow, and the rest of the course builds on them:

- It is **tool-using and stateful.** It can read your repo and run commands, so what it does
  depends on what it can see and what you let it do (permissions — that is `U3`).
- It is **non-deterministic.** Ask twice, get two differently-worded answers, sometimes two
  different approaches. So you never verify *wording*; you verify *behavior and the diff.*

**Verification is the through-line (CV).** The single habit that separates an elite operator from
someone who got burned by AI is this: **green tests are necessary, not sufficient — read the
diff.** A passing suite tells you nothing broke that was already tested; it does not tell you the
change is the one you wanted, or that it didn't quietly touch something else. Every lab in this
course ends with an explicit verification step. This unit's is the smallest possible version of
it, on purpose: one line, one diff, one check.

**The REPL.** {{vd:cli-basics}} You will live in the interactive session for daily work; the
non-interactive (`-p`) form is what `doctor` uses under the hood and what `U16` automates.

**Your baseline configuration (established here, taught later).** This unit drops a known-good
starter [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md) and a minimal
`.claude/settings.json` into the practice codebase so every later lab starts from the same place.
We are only *establishing* the baseline now — what project memory and `{{vd:settings}}` /
`{{vd:memory}}` actually do, and how to shape them, is the job of `U4` (Memory & context). Don't
edit them yet.

**Awareness — IDE integration.** Claude Code also runs inside your editor: {{vd:ide}} The course
labs are written for the terminal so they're portable, but the IDE path is fully supported; reach
for it if that's where you live. (We don't drill it.)

**Version currency.** This content was verified against Claude Code `{{vd:_verified_version}}`. If
your installed version differs, `doctor` will say so and point you at
[`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md), where any version-specific
differences are documented.

## Worked example

The preflight is itself an **authentic dogfooding artifact** (R14): [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor)
is the very script this course's maintainers run, and it is also a worked example of *scripting
Claude Code* — its auth check drives the CLI headlessly and inspects the result, the same pattern
`U16` generalizes. It reports pass/fail for four things, in order:

1. **install** — `claude` is on your `PATH`.
2. **version** — `claude --version` parses, and is compared to the recorded verified version.
3. **auth + first command** — it runs one real non-interactive command and checks it round-trips,
   which proves authentication actually works (not just that a token exists).
4. **tooling deps** — the course's own check suite can run.

Run it:

```bash
tools/doctor              # all checks (step 3 makes one real call)
tools/doctor --no-probe   # skip the network round-trip (offline / CI)
tools/doctor --manual     # also print the manual checklist fallback
```

If any check can't run, the same script prints a **manual checklist** so you are never blocked by
the tool meant to unblock you.

## Lab

**Goal:** land your first verified change — have Claude add a `service` field to the `/health`
endpoint of `taskflow-api`, so it returns `{"status": "ok", "service": "taskflow-api"}` — and
prove it, by reading the diff and running the check.

**Starting state:** `start/u01-lab1` (run `tools/reset-lab u01-lab1` to restore it). It is the
clean, green primary codebase plus your baseline config — no bug is planted; the "win" is the
addition.

**Steps:**

1. Run `tools/doctor`. Do not start the lab until it is green (or you've walked the manual
   checklist). An auth wall mid-task is the most common way a first session stalls.
2. Start an interactive session from inside the codebase:
   `cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api && claude`.
3. Orient first (this is the loop's "observe"): ask Claude *"What does the `/health` endpoint
   return today, and which file defines it?"* Confirm its answer against the file yourself.
4. Make the change: ask Claude to *"add a `service` field set to `taskflow-api` to the `/health`
   response, as a one-line change."*
5. **Read the diff before you accept it** (see *Verify* below). Then accept and commit it.

**Self-check (objective):** run `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. It passes only if the full pytest
suite is still green **and** `GET /health` now returns `"service": "taskflow-api"` alongside
`"status": "ok"`.

**Reference solution:** branch `solution/u01-lab1` — attempt the lab unaided first, then diff your
result against it.

**Verify (CV):** before accepting, read the proposed diff and confirm three things with your own
eyes: (a) it touches **only** the `/health` return line, (b) it *adds* the `service` field without
dropping the existing `"status": "ok"`, and (c) nothing else in the file moved. Then run the
self-check. This 30-second habit — not the passing check — is the actual skill this unit teaches.

> **Safety note.** This change is deliberately tiny and fully reversible (`git restore` /
> `git checkout` undoes it). Reviewing the diff *before* accepting is the safe-by-default path you
> will use for every change from here on, including the large ones where it matters far more.

## Common pitfalls

- **Accepting the edit without reading the diff.** The entire point of a deliberately trivial
  first task is to build the verify-the-diff reflex while the stakes are zero. Don't skip it here;
  you'll regret skipping it on a 200-line change later.
- **Skipping `doctor` and hitting an auth wall mid-lab.** Run the preflight first; a failed auth
  three steps into a task is disorienting and easy to misread as "Claude broke."
- **Expecting deterministic output.** Claude may phrase the change or its explanation differently
  than this page or than your last run. That's normal — verify the *resulting behavior and diff*,
  never the wording.
- **Treating the baseline config as homework.** The shipped `CLAUDE.md` / `settings.json` are
  established for you on purpose. Resist tuning them now; `U4` is where that pays off.

## Going deeper

- **Next:** U2 (Explore a codebase) puts Claude to work understanding code you've never seen; U3
  (Operate safely) makes the permission/blast-radius model explicit; U4 (Memory & context)
  explains the baseline config you just established.
- [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor) — read the script; it's short, and it's your model for
  the headless pattern in U16.
- [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) — where version drift is recorded if
  your CLI differs from the verified version.
- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).
</content>
</invoke>

- It is **non-deterministic.** Ask twice, get two differently-worded answers, sometimes two
  different approaches. So you never verify *wording*; you verify *behavior and the diff.*

**Verification is the through-line — we tag it CV (cross-cutting verification) in every unit.**
The single habit that separates an elite operator from someone who got burned by AI is this:
**green tests are necessary, not sufficient — read the diff.** A passing suite tells you nothing broke that was already tested; it does not tell you the
change is the one you wanted, or that it didn't quietly touch something else. Every lab in this
course ends with an explicit verification step. This unit's is the smallest possible version of
it, on purpose: one line, one diff, one check.

**The REPL.** {{vd:cli-basics}} You will live in the interactive session for daily work; the
non-interactive (`-p`) form is what `doctor` uses under the hood and what `U16` automates.

**Your baseline configuration (established here, taught later).** This unit drops a known-good
starter [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md) and a minimal
`.claude/settings.json` into the practice codebase so every later lab starts from the same place.
We are only *establishing* the baseline now — what project memory and `{{vd:settings}}` /
`{{vd:memory}}` actually do, and how to shape them, is the job of `U4` (Memory & context). Don't
edit them yet.

**Awareness — IDE integration.** Claude Code also runs inside your editor: {{vd:ide}} The course
labs are written for the terminal so they're portable, but the IDE path is fully supported; reach
for it if that's where you live. (We don't drill it.)

**Version currency.** This content was verified against Claude Code `{{vd:_verified_version}}`. If
your installed version differs, `doctor` will say so and point you at

differences are documented.

## Worked example

The preflight is itself an **authentic dogfooding artifact** (R14): [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor)
is the very script this course's maintainers run, and it is also a worked example of *scripting
Claude Code* — its auth check drives the CLI headlessly and inspects the result, the same pattern
`U16` generalizes. It reports pass/fail for four things, in order:

1. **install** — `claude` is on your `PATH`.
2. **version** — `claude --version` parses, and is compared to the recorded verified version.
3. **auth + first command** — it runs one real non-interactive command and checks it round-trips,
   which proves authentication actually works (not just that a token exists).
4. **tooling deps** — the course's own check suite can run.

Run it:

```bash

tools/doctor --no-probe   # skip the network round-trip (offline / CI)

```

the tool meant to unblock you.

## Lab

**Goal:** land your first verified change — have Claude add a `service` field to the `/health`
endpoint of `taskflow-api`, so it returns `{"status": "ok", "service": "taskflow-api"}` — and
prove it, by reading the diff and running the check.

clean, green primary codebase plus your baseline config — no bug is planted; the "win" is the
addition.

   checklist). An auth wall mid-task is the most common way a first session stalls.
2. Start an interactive session from inside the codebase:
   `cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api && claude`.
3. Orient first (this is the loop's "observe"): ask Claude *"What does the `/health` endpoint
   return today, and which file defines it?"* Confirm its answer against the file yourself.
4. Make the change: ask Claude to *"add a `service` field set to `taskflow-api` to the `/health`
   response, as a one-line change."*
5. **Read the diff before you accept it** (see *Verify* below). Then accept and commit it.

result against it.

  established for you on purpose. Resist tuning them now; `U4` is where that pays off.

content.description: For each mutating lab (u01,u05,u06,u07,u09,u11) + u03: confirm start/ tag fails-clean and solution/ branch passes verify.sh. Catalog any breakage.

content.subject: Lens 2: Substrate code quality
content.description: Run taskflow-api pytest, taskflow-cli checks, tools/doctor, make check. Review substrate realism/cleanliness.

content.subject: Lens 3: Content accuracy & version-currency
content.description: Spot-check CLI claims vs installed CLI; verify no memory-authored facts; confirm L1 unverified keys honestly marked.

content.subject: Lens 4: Pedagogical quality (terseness focus)
content.description: Read units as a learner: altitude for senior-but-AI-novice, terseness/density, no SWE basics, stage progression, woven CV.

content.subject: Lens 5: Consistency & conventions
content.description: Naming, structure, tone, glossary terms, front-matter uniformity, tier-template adherence.

content.subject: Lens 6: Learner experience & ID/notation overload
content.description: Onboarding->stuck->checklist->capstone flow; through-lines; whether spec-internal IDs (R/U/§/C/W/L) leak into learner-facing prose.

content.subject: Lens 7: Authenticity (dogfooding R14)
content.description: Referenced artifacts genuinely used & accurate; CLAUDE.md accurate; case study honest.

content.subject: Lens 8: Accessibility (R15)
content.description: CommonMark, no meaning by color/emoji alone, text equivalents, WSL story.

content.subject: Compile severity-ranked findings report
content.description: Aggregate all lens findings into one report; fix nothing.

# Get set up and land your first verified win

## Learning objectives

By the end of this unit you can:

- **Install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code setup with a single repeatable
  command** (the `doctor` preflight).
- **Explain the agentic loop in one breath and drive a single, verified one-line change into a
  real codebase.**

## Fast path (TL;DR)

> Run [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor). When it is green, open an interactive session in
> `codebases/primary/taskflow-api/`, ask Claude to add a `service` field to the `/health`
> response, **read the one-line diff before accepting it**, then run
> `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. Green check = first win. That is the whole unit.

## Skip-check

**Skip this unit if you can already:** install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code
setup with a repeatable check; and explain what the agentic loop is while landing a single,
*verified* one-line change against a real codebase (verified by you reading the diff and a check
passing — not by trusting that Claude said it was done).

## Concept

You are an experienced engineer; this unit does not teach the terminal or Git. It teaches the two
things that are genuinely new when you add Claude Code to your workflow: **how the tool operates**
and **why verification is now your job, explicitly.**

**The agentic loop.** Claude Code is not autocomplete. It runs a loop: it **observes** (reads
files, runs commands, inspects output), **acts** (proposes an edit, runs a command), and then the
**result feeds back in** — and it repeats until the goal is met or it hands back to you. Two
consequences follow, and the rest of the course builds on them:

- It is **tool-using and stateful.** It can read your repo and run commands, so what it does
  depends on what it can see and what you let it do (permissions — the subject of **Operate
  safely**, a few units on).
- It is **non-deterministic.** Ask twice, get two differently-worded answers, sometimes two
  different approaches. So you never verify *wording*; you verify *behavior and the diff.*

**Verification is the through-line — we tag it CV (cross-cutting verification) in every unit.**
The single habit that separates an elite operator from someone who got burned by AI is this:
**green tests are necessary, not sufficient — read the diff.** A passing suite tells you nothing broke that was already tested; it does not tell you the
change is the one you wanted, or that it didn't quietly touch something else. Every lab in this
course ends with an explicit verification step. This unit's is the smallest possible version of
it, on purpose: one line, one diff, one check.

**The REPL.** {{vd:cli-basics}} You will live in the interactive session for daily work; the
non-interactive (`-p`) form is what `doctor` uses under the hood and what the final unit,
**Automate & scale**, builds on.

**Your baseline configuration (established here, taught later).** This unit drops a known-good
starter [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md) and a minimal
`.claude/settings.json` into the practice codebase so every later lab starts from the same place.
We're only *establishing* the baseline now. What project memory and `{{vd:settings}}` /
`{{vd:memory}}` actually do — and how to shape them — is the job of the **Memory & context** unit.
Don't edit them yet.

**Awareness — IDE integration.** Claude Code also runs inside your editor: {{vd:ide}} The course
labs are written for the terminal so they're portable, but the IDE path is fully supported; reach
for it if that's where you live. (We don't drill it.)

**Version currency.** This content was verified against Claude Code `{{vd:_verified_version}}`. If
your installed version differs, `doctor` will say so and point you at
[`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md), where any version-specific
differences are documented.

## Worked example

The preflight is itself an **authentic dogfooding artifact**: [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor)
is the very script this course's maintainers run, and it is also a worked example of *scripting
Claude Code* — its auth check drives the CLI headlessly and inspects the result, the same pattern
the final unit generalizes. It reports pass/fail for four things, in order:

1. **install** — `claude` is on your `PATH`.
2. **version** — `claude --version` parses, and is compared to the recorded verified version.
3. **auth + first command** — it runs one real non-interactive command and checks it round-trips,
   which proves authentication actually works (not just that a token exists).
4. **tooling deps** — the course's own check suite can run.

Run it:

```bash
tools/doctor              # all checks (step 3 makes one real call)
tools/doctor --no-probe   # skip the network round-trip (offline / CI)
tools/doctor --manual     # also print the manual checklist fallback
```

If any check can't run, the same script prints a **manual checklist** so you are never blocked by
the tool meant to unblock you.

## Lab

**Goal:** land your first verified change — have Claude add a `service` field to the `/health`
endpoint of `taskflow-api`, so it returns `{"status": "ok", "service": "taskflow-api"}` — and
prove it, by reading the diff and running the check.

**Starting state:** `start/u01-lab1` (run `tools/reset-lab u01-lab1` to restore it). It is the
clean, green primary codebase plus your baseline config — no bug is planted; the "win" is the
addition.

**Steps:**

1. Run `tools/doctor`. Do not start the lab until it is green (or you've walked the manual
   checklist). An auth wall mid-task is the most common way a first session stalls.
2. Start an interactive session from inside the codebase:
   `cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api && claude`.
3. Orient first (this is the loop's "observe"): ask Claude *"What does the `/health` endpoint
   return today, and which file defines it?"* Confirm its answer against the file yourself.
4. Make the change: ask Claude to *"add a `service` field set to `taskflow-api` to the `/health`
   response, as a one-line change."*
5. **Read the diff before you accept it** (see *Verify* below). Then accept and commit it.

**Self-check (objective):** run `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. It passes only if the full pytest
suite is still green **and** `GET /health` now returns `"service": "taskflow-api"` alongside
`"status": "ok"`.

**Reference solution:** branch `solution/u01-lab1` — attempt the lab unaided first, then diff your
result against it.

**Verify (CV):** before accepting, read the proposed diff and confirm three things with your own
eyes: (a) it touches **only** the `/health` return line, (b) it *adds* the `service` field without
dropping the existing `"status": "ok"`, and (c) nothing else in the file moved. Then run the
self-check. This 30-second habit — not the passing check — is the actual skill this unit teaches.

> **Safety note.** This change is deliberately tiny and fully reversible (`git restore` /
> `git checkout` undoes it). Reviewing the diff *before* accepting is the safe-by-default path you
> will use for every change from here on, including the large ones where it matters far more.

## Common pitfalls

- **Accepting the edit without reading the diff.** The entire point of a deliberately trivial
  first task is to build the verify-the-diff reflex while the stakes are zero. Don't skip it here;
  you'll regret skipping it on a 200-line change later.
- **Skipping `doctor` and hitting an auth wall mid-lab.** Run the preflight first; a failed auth
  three steps into a task is disorienting and easy to misread as "Claude broke."
- **Expecting deterministic output.** Claude may phrase the change or its explanation differently
  than this page or than your last run. That's normal — verify the *resulting behavior and diff*,
  never the wording.
- **Treating the baseline config as homework.** The shipped `CLAUDE.md` / `settings.json` are
  established for you on purpose. Resist tuning them now; `U4` is where that pays off.

## Going deeper

- **Next:** U2 (Explore a codebase) puts Claude to work understanding code you've never seen; U3
  (Operate safely) makes the permission/blast-radius model explicit; U4 (Memory & context)
  explains the baseline config you just established.
- [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor) — read the script; it's short, and it's your model for
  the headless pattern in U16.
- [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) — where version drift is recorded if
  your CLI differs from the verified version.
- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).
</content>
</invoke>

content.text:
**Pilot done — paused for your sign-off.** Here's the U1 voice, before → after:

**M2 — codes become titles (the core fix):**
> *"(permissions — that is `U3`)"* → *"(permissions — the subject of **Operate safely**, a few units on)"*
>
> *"what `U16` automates"* → *"what the final unit, **Automate & scale**, builds on"*
>
> *"the job of `U4` (Memory & context)"* → *"the job of the **Memory & context** unit"*
>
> Going-deeper: *"U2 (Explore a codebase) … U3 (Operate safely) … U4 (Memory & context)"* → *"**Explore a codebase** … **Operate safely** … **Memory & context**. (These are the next three units, in order.)"*

**M1 — requirement-ID dropped:**
> *"an **authentic dogfooding artifact** (R14):"* → *"an **authentic dogfooding artifact**:"*

**M2 — redundant code tails dropped from objectives** (the `can_do: [C1, C2]` front matter already carries this for the checklist):
> *"…(the `doctor` preflight) — advances `C1`."* → *"…(the `doctor` preflight)."*

**L3 — `CV` expanded on first use:**
> *"**Verification is the through-line (CV).**"* → *"**Verification is the through-line — we tag it CV (cross-cutting verification) in every unit.**"*

**L1 — light density relief** (one run-on split):
> *"…starts from the same place. We are only *establishing* the baseline now — what project memory and … actually do, and how to shape them, is the job of `U4` (Memory & context)."* → *"…starts from the same place. We're only *establishing* the baseline now. What project memory and … actually do — and how to shape them — is the job of the **Memory & context** unit."*

Net effect: the taxonomy codes that forced you to remember "what's U3 vs R14" are gone from prose; cross-refs now read as plain unit names; `CV` is defined where introduced. `make check` green, traceability intact (front-matter `can_do`/`requirements` and rubric `[Cn]` tags untouched by design).

Two calibration questions before I roll this across U2–U16:

questions.question: Is the U1 voice right to roll across all 16 units?

options.label: Yes — roll it as-is
options.description: Apply this same M1/M2/L3 treatment + light L1 touches across U2–U16 and the cross-cutting files, plus the L2 reading-time fixes. I'll re-run make check per batch.

options.label: Yes, but go heavier on L1
options.description: Same code/ID cleanup, but be more aggressive on density — actively rewrite clipped Concept passages into longer, more breathing prose (will raise reading times somewhat).

options.label: Lighter touch
options.description: Do M1 (R# strip) + L2 + L3 everywhere, but leave M2 cross-ref codes mostly alone except where clearly redundant. Less churn, keeps more of the existing terseness.

options.label: Title only
options.description: "the Operate safely unit" / "Explore a codebase". Cleanest prose; no numbers. (What the U1 pilot does.)

options.label: Title + number
options.description: "Operate safely (Unit 3)". Keeps a navigational number without the bare-code feel. Slightly more verbose.

options.label: Number-forward
options.description: "Unit 3 (Operate safely)". Number first — easiest to locate in the units/ directory, but reintroduces some of the numeric friction you flagged.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Quality-pass remediation checklist (2026-05-30)

**Origin:** post-v1 systematic quality pass (8 lenses; functional + content). Mechanical gate, lab
integrity, substrates, version system, authenticity, accessibility all verified **clean** — findings
are confined to learner-facing prose ergonomics. **This is polish, not breakage; nothing here blocks
release.** This file is the spanning tracker so the work survives session boundaries (continuity
hygiene). Tracked as open-loop **L8** in `decisions.md`.

## Findings → remediation

- **M1 — strip spec requirement-IDs (`R#`/`R#.AC#`) from learner prose.** No learner-facing key
  exists for `R#`; traceability already lives in unit front-matter `requirements:` + the checks.
  **Keep the one teaching exception** (U10 `Concept` uses `R1`/`R2.AC3` to *illustrate* what
  requirement IDs look like). Demote any genuinely useful maintainer cite to an HTML comment.
- **M2 — rephrase bare taxonomy codes (`U#`, `C#`, `W#`, `CV`) into human language.** Prefer
  title-first ("**Operate safely**" not "`U3`"); drop codes that merely echo adjacent prose; keep
  codes only where load-bearing + defined (front matter, progress-checklist, rubric, glossary).
- **L1 — relieve density in the most compressed `Concept` passages.** Let key ideas land in their
  own sentence; lighten bold-fragment/em-dash/semicolon pile-ups. Light touch — do **not** bloat
  reading time. (Prose is generally good; this targets specific dense spots, e.g. U1.)
- **L2 — recompute `reading_time_min`.** Under-declared on heavier units (U13 8→~12, U14 8→~11,
  U12 8→~10). Reading time only (lab time is separate). Mechanical.
- **L3 — expand `CV` on first use** per unit (cross-cutting verification), then the code is fine.

## Per-unit grid

Legend: `R#` = has requirement-ID leak (M1) · all units need M2/L3 sweep · `rt` = reading_time fix (L2).

| Unit | M1 (R#) | M2 (codes) | L1 (density) | L2 (rt) | L3 (CV) | done |
|------|---------|-----------|--------------|---------|---------|------|
| U1 onboarding        | yes (R14) | yes | yes (Concept) | ok (8/~6) | yes | ⬜ PILOT |
| U2 explore           | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U3 operate-safely    | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U4 memory-context    | yes (R14×2) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U5 ship-feature      | — | yes | ok (good prose) | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U6 tdd               | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U7 debug             | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U8 git-pr            | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U9 legacy-refactor   | yes (R7.AC3) | yes | review | ok (11/~12) | yes | ⬜ |
| U10 spec-driven      | yes (keep teaching R1/R2.AC3; strip R3.AC2/R7.AC3) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U11 review           | yes (R7.AC3) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U12 commands-skills  | yes (R14, R7.AC3×2) | yes | review | **rt 8→10** | yes | ⬜ |
| U13 subagents        | yes (R7.AC3×2) | yes | review | **rt 8→12** | yes | ⬜ |
| U14 hooks            | yes (R14, R13×2) | yes | review | **rt 8→11** | yes | ⬜ |
| U15 mcp-vetting      | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U16 automate-scale   | yes (R14, R12, R15) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |

**Cross-cutting files to sweep after units:** `course/capstone/{README,briefs,rubric,case-study}.md`,
`course/stuck.md`, `course/maintainer-guide.md` (maintainer-facing — `R#`/codes may be *appropriate*
here; sweep with judgment, don't strip load-bearing rubric `[Cn]` tags).

**Verification after each batch:** `make check` (links/refs/traceability must stay green — none of
these edits touch front-matter `requirements:`/`can_do:` or the rubric `[Cn]` tags, so traceability
is unaffected by design).

## Status

- [ ] U1 pilot complete → **PAUSE for voice approval** (current step)
- [ ] U2–U16 rolled after approval
- [ ] Cross-cutting files swept
- [ ] `make check` green; reading times recomputed
- [ ] L8 struck through in `decisions.md`; IMPLEMENTATION §3 noted

message.content: First is the table at the top of each unit. can_do just shows C1 and C2, which as plain text non-links mean nothing to me as a new learner. coverage_areas shows 1 and 27, again not links so I can't learn what that means. We should put None for prerequisites if none are required.

content.text:
Good eye — that block is the YAML **front matter**, and GitHub renders it as a table, so a learner really does see `can_do: [C1, C2]` and `coverage_areas: [1, 27]` as bare, meaningless tokens. But there's a real constraint here: that front matter is **machine-readable by design** (R6) and the enforcement checks *consume* those exact fields — `check-traceability` reads `can_do`, `check-coverage` reads `coverage_areas`, `check-frontmatter` validates the whole thing against a schema. So I need to see what's actually changeable before touching it, or I'll break `make check` across all 16 units.

Let me look at the schema and the consumers:

content.text:
The schema is strict and machine-bound: `can_do` must be `C#`/`CV` strings (consumed by `check-traceability` + `check-coverage`), `coverage_areas` must be integers (consumed by `check-coverage`), and `prerequisites` must be an array of `U#` strings — so I literally **cannot** write `prerequisites: None` or human text into those fields without breaking `make check` across all units and violating the R6 machine-readable-front-matter design.

So the front matter has to stay as the *machine* layer. The clean reconciliation is a **learner-facing "At a glance" block in the body** (right under the title) that translates the metadata into plain, linked language — including "Prerequisites: None". Let me pull the real coverage-area names and check the unit template so any change generalizes properly:

# find structure
def walk(o,depth=0):

print(type(d), list(d.keys())[:5] if isinstance(d,dict) else len(d))
PY

content.text: Good question — let me answer it factually rather than guess. Whether the block *must* be at the top depends on two things: how our own `check-frontmatter` parser finds it, and how GitHub renders it. Let me check the parser:

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""check-frontmatter (R13.AC4a): every authored unit's YAML front matter validates against
3	meta/unit-frontmatter.schema.json, and a few cross-file invariants hold (id is unique and known
4	to the catalog; declared can_do / coverage_areas / workflows reference real entries).
5	
6	Vacuously green until P5 authors units — exactly what we want from a guard built before content.
7	"""
8	import json
9	import os
10	import sys
11	
12	sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))
13	from _common import META, Reporter, load_yaml, parse_frontmatter, unit_files  # noqa: E402
14	
15	import jsonschema  # noqa: E402
16	
17	
18	def main(argv):
19	    rep = Reporter("check-frontmatter")
20	    schema = json.loads((META / "unit-frontmatter.schema.json").read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
21	    validator = jsonschema.Draft202012Validator(schema)
22	
23	    catalog = load_yaml(META / "use-case-catalog.yaml")
24	    cap = load_yaml(META / "capability-map.yaml")
25	    cov = load_yaml(META / "coverage-matrix.yaml")
26	    known_units = {u["unit"] for u in catalog["use_cases"]}
27	    known_caps = {c["id"] for c in cap["capabilities"]}
28	    known_areas = {a["id"] for a in cov["areas"]}
29	    valid_workflows = {f"W{i}" for i in range(1, 10)}
30	
31	    files = unit_files()
32	    seen_ids = {}
33	    for path in files:
34	        rel = path.relative_to(META.parent)
35	        fm = parse_frontmatter(path)
36	        if fm is None:
37	            rep.fail(f"{rel}: no YAML front matter found")
38	            continue
39	        errors = sorted(validator.iter_errors(fm), key=lambda e: e.path)
40	        if errors:
41	            for e in errors:
42	                loc = "/".join(str(p) for p in e.path) or "(root)"
43	                rep.fail(f"{rel}: {loc}: {e.message}")
44	            continue
45	        uid = fm["id"]
46	        if uid in seen_ids:
47	            rep.fail(f"{rel}: duplicate unit id {uid} (also in {seen_ids[uid]})")
48	        seen_ids[uid] = rel
49	        if uid not in known_units:
50	            rep.fail(f"{rel}: id {uid} is not in the use-case catalog")
51	        bad_caps = set(fm["can_do"]) - known_caps
52	        bad_areas = set(fm["coverage_areas"]) - known_areas
53	        bad_wf = set(fm["workflows"]) - valid_workflows
54	        bad_prereq = set(fm["prerequisites"]) - known_units
55	        if bad_caps:
56	            rep.fail(f"{rel}: unknown can_do ids {sorted(bad_caps)}")
57	        if bad_areas:
58	            rep.fail(f"{rel}: unknown coverage_areas {sorted(bad_areas)}")
59	        if bad_wf:
60	            rep.fail(f"{rel}: unknown workflow ids {sorted(bad_wf)}")
61	        if bad_prereq:
62	            rep.fail(f"{rel}: unknown prerequisite unit ids {sorted(bad_prereq)}")
63	        if not rep.failures:
64	            rep.ok(f"{rel}: front matter valid ({uid})")
65	
66	    if not files:
67	        rep.ok("no units authored yet — nothing to validate (guard is in place)")
68	    return rep.done()
69	
70	
71	if __name__ == "__main__":
72	    raise SystemExit(main(sys.argv[1:]))
73

content.text:
Here's the factual answer to "must it be at the top?":

**Yes, effectively — for three independent reasons:**

1. **Our own checker requires it.** `parse_frontmatter` (in `tools/_common.py`) does `FRONTMATTER_RE.match(text)` — `.match` is anchored to the **start** of the file (it only tolerates a leading HTML comment before it). Move the block to the bottom and the parser returns `None` → `check-frontmatter` fails with "no YAML front matter found" for every unit. We'd have to rewrite the parser.
2. **GitHub only renders the table at the top.** The nice key/value table appears *only* when YAML front matter leads the file. Put it at the bottom and GitHub renders the `---` fences as horizontal rules and the YAML as raw `key: value` body text — messier, not cleaner.
3. **It's the convention the course itself teaches and dogfoods.** R6 literally calls it "front matter"; relocating it would make our own example contradict what the curriculum says front matter is.

So relocation isn't a good lever. The honest options are:

| Option | What it does | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **A. "At a glance" block** *(recommended)* | Leave the YAML untouched as the machine layer; add a short human block right under the `# Title` — **Prerequisites: None**, the can-dos in plain linked language, coverage areas by **name**, read/lab time. | Light. Template + 16 units. No schema/tooling change. `make check` stays green. The YAML table still renders above on GitHub, but it's clearly metadata and the human version sits right below. |
| **B. Sidecar metadata file** | Move the machine fields out of `unit.md` into a sibling `meta.yaml`; the learner's `unit.md` opens with just title + prose, no table. | Heavy. Rewrites the schema, all checks, templates, all 16 units — and **contradicts the R6 design decision** that metadata lives in unit front matter. I'd advise against it. |

My recommendation is **A**: it gives you exactly what you asked for — `Prerequisites: None`, meaningful coverage names, readable can-dos — in the layer the learner actually reads, without fighting the machine contract. The unavoidable residue is that GitHub still shows the small YAML table; but an experienced dev reads that as "metadata, skip" and the human block is immediately below.

Want me to draft the "At a glance" block on U1 so you can see it? Or if the GitHub table itself is the real irritant, we can talk through B with eyes open about what it costs.

# Get set up and land your first verified win

## Learning objectives

By the end of this unit you can:

- **Install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code setup with a single repeatable
  command** (the `doctor` preflight).
- **Explain the agentic loop in one breath and drive a single, verified one-line change into a
  real codebase.**

## Fast path (TL;DR)

> Run [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor). When it is green, open an interactive session in
> `codebases/primary/taskflow-api/`, ask Claude to add a `service` field to the `/health`
> response, **read the one-line diff before accepting it**, then run
> `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. Green check = first win. That is the whole unit.

## Skip-check

**Skip this unit if you can already:** install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code
setup with a repeatable check; and explain what the agentic loop is while landing a single,
*verified* one-line change against a real codebase (verified by you reading the diff and a check
passing — not by trusting that Claude said it was done).

## Concept

You are an experienced engineer; this unit does not teach the terminal or Git. It teaches the two
things that are genuinely new when you add Claude Code to your workflow: **how the tool operates**
and **why verification is now your job, explicitly.**

**The agentic loop.** Claude Code is not autocomplete. It runs a loop: it **observes** (reads
files, runs commands, inspects output), **acts** (proposes an edit, runs a command), and then the
**result feeds back in** — and it repeats until the goal is met or it hands back to you. Two
consequences follow, and the rest of the course builds on them:

- It is **tool-using and stateful.** It can read your repo and run commands, so what it does
  depends on what it can see and what you let it do (permissions — the subject of **Operate
  safely**, a few units on).
- It is **non-deterministic.** Ask twice, get two differently-worded answers, sometimes two
  different approaches. So you never verify *wording*; you verify *behavior and the diff.*

**Verification is the through-line — we tag it CV (cross-cutting verification) in every unit.**
The single habit that separates an elite operator from someone who got burned by AI is this:
**green tests are necessary, not sufficient — read the diff.** A passing suite tells you nothing broke that was already tested; it does not tell you the
change is the one you wanted, or that it didn't quietly touch something else. Every lab in this
course ends with an explicit verification step. This unit's is the smallest possible version of
it, on purpose: one line, one diff, one check.

**The REPL.** {{vd:cli-basics}} You will live in the interactive session for daily work; the
non-interactive (`-p`) form is what `doctor` uses under the hood and what the final unit,
**Automate & scale**, builds on.

**Your baseline configuration (established here, taught later).** This unit drops a known-good
starter [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md) and a minimal
`.claude/settings.json` into the practice codebase so every later lab starts from the same place.
We're only *establishing* the baseline now. What project memory and `{{vd:settings}}` /
`{{vd:memory}}` actually do — and how to shape them — is the job of the **Memory & context** unit.
Don't edit them yet.

**Awareness — IDE integration.** Claude Code also runs inside your editor: {{vd:ide}} The course
labs are written for the terminal so they're portable, but the IDE path is fully supported; reach
for it if that's where you live. (We don't drill it.)

**Version currency.** This content was verified against Claude Code `{{vd:_verified_version}}`. If
your installed version differs, `doctor` will say so and point you at
[`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md), where any version-specific
differences are documented.

## Worked example

The preflight is itself an **authentic dogfooding artifact**: [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor)
is the very script this course's maintainers run, and it is also a worked example of *scripting
Claude Code* — its auth check drives the CLI headlessly and inspects the result, the same pattern
the final unit generalizes. It reports pass/fail for four things, in order:

1. **install** — `claude` is on your `PATH`.
2. **version** — `claude --version` parses, and is compared to the recorded verified version.
3. **auth + first command** — it runs one real non-interactive command and checks it round-trips,
   which proves authentication actually works (not just that a token exists).
4. **tooling deps** — the course's own check suite can run.

Run it:

```bash
tools/doctor              # all checks (step 3 makes one real call)
tools/doctor --no-probe   # skip the network round-trip (offline / CI)
tools/doctor --manual     # also print the manual checklist fallback
```

If any check can't run, the same script prints a **manual checklist** so you are never blocked by
the tool meant to unblock you.

## Lab

**Goal:** land your first verified change — have Claude add a `service` field to the `/health`
endpoint of `taskflow-api`, so it returns `{"status": "ok", "service": "taskflow-api"}` — and
prove it, by reading the diff and running the check.

**Starting state:** `start/u01-lab1` (run `tools/reset-lab u01-lab1` to restore it). It is the
clean, green primary codebase plus your baseline config — no bug is planted; the "win" is the
addition.

**Steps:**

1. Run `tools/doctor`. Do not start the lab until it is green (or you've walked the manual
   checklist). An auth wall mid-task is the most common way a first session stalls.
2. Start an interactive session from inside the codebase:
   `cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api && claude`.
3. Orient first (this is the loop's "observe"): ask Claude *"What does the `/health` endpoint
   return today, and which file defines it?"* Confirm its answer against the file yourself.
4. Make the change: ask Claude to *"add a `service` field set to `taskflow-api` to the `/health`
   response, as a one-line change."*
5. **Read the diff before you accept it** (see *Verify* below). Then accept and commit it.

**Self-check (objective):** run `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. It passes only if the full pytest
suite is still green **and** `GET /health` now returns `"service": "taskflow-api"` alongside
`"status": "ok"`.

**Reference solution:** branch `solution/u01-lab1` — attempt the lab unaided first, then diff your
result against it.

**Verify (CV):** before accepting, read the proposed diff and confirm three things with your own
eyes: (a) it touches **only** the `/health` return line, (b) it *adds* the `service` field without
dropping the existing `"status": "ok"`, and (c) nothing else in the file moved. Then run the
self-check. This 30-second habit — not the passing check — is the actual skill this unit teaches.

> **Safety note.** This change is deliberately tiny and fully reversible (`git restore` /
> `git checkout` undoes it). Reviewing the diff *before* accepting is the safe-by-default path you
> will use for every change from here on, including the large ones where it matters far more.

## Common pitfalls

- **Accepting the edit without reading the diff.** The entire point of a deliberately trivial
  first task is to build the verify-the-diff reflex while the stakes are zero. Don't skip it here;
  you'll regret skipping it on a 200-line change later.
- **Skipping `doctor` and hitting an auth wall mid-lab.** Run the preflight first; a failed auth
  three steps into a task is disorienting and easy to misread as "Claude broke."
- **Expecting deterministic output.** Claude may phrase the change or its explanation differently
  than this page or than your last run. That's normal — verify the *resulting behavior and diff*,
  never the wording.
- **Treating the baseline config as homework.** The shipped `CLAUDE.md` / `settings.json` are
  established for you on purpose. Resist tuning them now; **Memory & context** is where that pays off.

## Going deeper

- **Next:** **Explore a codebase** puts Claude to work understanding code you've never seen;
  **Operate safely** makes the permission/blast-radius model explicit; **Memory & context**
  explains the baseline config you just established. (These are the next three units, in order.)
- [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor) — read the script; it's short, and it's your model for
  the headless pattern in the final unit.
- [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) — where version drift is recorded if
  your CLI differs from the verified version.
- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).
</content>

# Get set up and land your first verified win

## Learning objectives

By the end of this unit you can:

- **Install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code setup with a single repeatable
  command** (the `doctor` preflight).
- **Explain the agentic loop in one breath and drive a single, verified one-line change into a
  real codebase.**

## Fast path (TL;DR)

> Run [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor). When it is green, open an interactive session in
> `codebases/primary/taskflow-api/`, ask Claude to add a `service` field to the `/health`
> response, **read the one-line diff before accepting it**, then run
> `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. Green check = first win. That is the whole unit.

## Skip-check

**Skip this unit if you can already:** install, authenticate, and confirm a working Claude Code
setup with a repeatable check; and explain what the agentic loop is while landing a single,
*verified* one-line change against a real codebase (verified by you reading the diff and a check
passing — not by trusting that Claude said it was done).

## Concept

You are an experienced engineer; this unit does not teach the terminal or Git. It teaches the two
things that are genuinely new when you add Claude Code to your workflow: **how the tool operates**
and **why verification is now your job, explicitly.**

**The agentic loop.** Claude Code is not autocomplete. It runs a loop: it **observes** (reads
files, runs commands, inspects output), **acts** (proposes an edit, runs a command), and then the
**result feeds back in** — and it repeats until the goal is met or it hands back to you. Two
consequences follow, and the rest of the course builds on them:

- It is **tool-using and stateful.** It can read your repo and run commands, so what it does
  depends on what it can see and what you let it do (permissions — the subject of **Operate
  safely**, a few units on).
- It is **non-deterministic.** Ask twice, get two differently-worded answers, sometimes two
  different approaches. So you never verify *wording*; you verify *behavior and the diff.*

**Verification is the through-line — we tag it CV (cross-cutting verification) in every unit.**
The single habit that separates an elite operator from someone who got burned by AI is this:
**green tests are necessary, not sufficient — read the diff.** A passing suite tells you nothing broke that was already tested; it does not tell you the
change is the one you wanted, or that it didn't quietly touch something else. Every lab in this
course ends with an explicit verification step. This unit's is the smallest possible version of
it, on purpose: one line, one diff, one check.

**The REPL.** {{vd:cli-basics}} You will live in the interactive session for daily work; the
non-interactive (`-p`) form is what `doctor` uses under the hood and what the final unit,
**Automate & scale**, builds on.

**Your baseline configuration (established here, taught later).** This unit drops a known-good
starter [`CLAUDE.md`](../../../codebases/primary/taskflow-api/CLAUDE.md) and a minimal
`.claude/settings.json` into the practice codebase so every later lab starts from the same place.
We're only *establishing* the baseline now. What project memory and `{{vd:settings}}` /
`{{vd:memory}}` actually do — and how to shape them — is the job of the **Memory & context** unit.
Don't edit them yet.

**Awareness — IDE integration.** Claude Code also runs inside your editor: {{vd:ide}} The course
labs are written for the terminal so they're portable, but the IDE path is fully supported; reach
for it if that's where you live. (We don't drill it.)

**Version currency.** This content was verified against Claude Code `{{vd:_verified_version}}`. If
your installed version differs, `doctor` will say so and point you at
[`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md), where any version-specific
differences are documented.

## Worked example

The preflight is itself an **authentic dogfooding artifact**: [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor)
is the very script this course's maintainers run, and it is also a worked example of *scripting
Claude Code* — its auth check drives the CLI headlessly and inspects the result, the same pattern
the final unit generalizes. It reports pass/fail for four things, in order:

1. **install** — `claude` is on your `PATH`.
2. **version** — `claude --version` parses, and is compared to the recorded verified version.
3. **auth + first command** — it runs one real non-interactive command and checks it round-trips,
   which proves authentication actually works (not just that a token exists).
4. **tooling deps** — the course's own check suite can run.

Run it:

```bash
tools/doctor              # all checks (step 3 makes one real call)
tools/doctor --no-probe   # skip the network round-trip (offline / CI)
tools/doctor --manual     # also print the manual checklist fallback
```

If any check can't run, the same script prints a **manual checklist** so you are never blocked by
the tool meant to unblock you.

## Lab

**Goal:** land your first verified change — have Claude add a `service` field to the `/health`
endpoint of `taskflow-api`, so it returns `{"status": "ok", "service": "taskflow-api"}` — and
prove it, by reading the diff and running the check.

**Starting state:** `start/u01-lab1` (run `tools/reset-lab u01-lab1` to restore it). It is the
clean, green primary codebase plus your baseline config — no bug is planted; the "win" is the
addition.

**Steps:**

1. Run `tools/doctor`. Do not start the lab until it is green (or you've walked the manual
   checklist). An auth wall mid-task is the most common way a first session stalls.
2. Start an interactive session from inside the codebase:
   `cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api && claude`.
3. Orient first (this is the loop's "observe"): ask Claude *"What does the `/health` endpoint
   return today, and which file defines it?"* Confirm its answer against the file yourself.
4. Make the change: ask Claude to *"add a `service` field set to `taskflow-api` to the `/health`
   response, as a one-line change."*
5. **Read the diff before you accept it** (see *Verify* below). Then accept and commit it.

**Self-check (objective):** run `tools/verify-lab u01-lab1`. It passes only if the full pytest
suite is still green **and** `GET /health` now returns `"service": "taskflow-api"` alongside
`"status": "ok"`.

**Reference solution:** branch `solution/u01-lab1` — attempt the lab unaided first, then diff your
result against it.

**Verify (CV):** before accepting, read the proposed diff and confirm three things with your own
eyes: (a) it touches **only** the `/health` return line, (b) it *adds* the `service` field without
dropping the existing `"status": "ok"`, and (c) nothing else in the file moved. Then run the
self-check. This 30-second habit — not the passing check — is the actual skill this unit teaches.

> **Safety note.** This change is deliberately tiny and fully reversible (`git restore` /
> `git checkout` undoes it). Reviewing the diff *before* accepting is the safe-by-default path you
> will use for every change from here on, including the large ones where it matters far more.

## Common pitfalls

- **Accepting the edit without reading the diff.** The entire point of a deliberately trivial
  first task is to build the verify-the-diff reflex while the stakes are zero. Don't skip it here;
  you'll regret skipping it on a 200-line change later.
- **Skipping `doctor` and hitting an auth wall mid-lab.** Run the preflight first; a failed auth
  three steps into a task is disorienting and easy to misread as "Claude broke."
- **Expecting deterministic output.** Claude may phrase the change or its explanation differently
  than this page or than your last run. That's normal — verify the *resulting behavior and diff*,
  never the wording.
- **Treating the baseline config as homework.** The shipped `CLAUDE.md` / `settings.json` are
  established for you on purpose. Resist tuning them now; **Memory & context** is where that pays off.

## Going deeper

- **Next:** **Explore a codebase** puts Claude to work understanding code you've never seen;
  **Operate safely** makes the permission/blast-radius model explicit; **Memory & context**
  explains the baseline config you just established. (These are the next three units, in order.)
- [`tools/doctor`](../../../tools/doctor) — read the script; it's short, and it's your model for
  the headless pattern in the final unit.
- [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md) — where version drift is recorded if
  your CLI differs from the verified version.
- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).
</content>

# Quality-pass remediation checklist (2026-05-30)

**Origin:** post-v1 systematic quality pass (8 lenses; functional + content). Mechanical gate, lab
integrity, substrates, version system, authenticity, accessibility all verified **clean** — findings
are confined to learner-facing prose ergonomics. **This is polish, not breakage; nothing here blocks
release.** This file is the spanning tracker so the work survives session boundaries (continuity
hygiene). Tracked as open-loop **L8** in `decisions.md`.

## Findings → remediation

- **M1 — strip spec requirement-IDs (`R#`/`R#.AC#`) from learner prose.** No learner-facing key
  exists for `R#`; traceability already lives in unit front-matter `requirements:` + the checks.
  **Keep the one teaching exception** (U10 `Concept` uses `R1`/`R2.AC3` to *illustrate* what
  requirement IDs look like). Demote any genuinely useful maintainer cite to an HTML comment.
- **M2 — rephrase bare taxonomy codes (`U#`, `C#`, `W#`, `CV`) into human language.** Prefer
  title-first ("**Operate safely**" not "`U3`"); drop codes that merely echo adjacent prose; keep
  codes only where load-bearing + defined (front matter, progress-checklist, rubric, glossary).
- **L1 — relieve density in the most compressed `Concept` passages.** Let key ideas land in their
  own sentence; lighten bold-fragment/em-dash/semicolon pile-ups. Light touch — do **not** bloat
  reading time. (Prose is generally good; this targets specific dense spots, e.g. U1.)
- **L2 — recompute `reading_time_min`.** Under-declared on heavier units (U13 8→~12, U14 8→~11,
  U12 8→~10). Reading time only (lab time is separate). Mechanical.
- **L3 — expand `CV` on first use** per unit (cross-cutting verification), then the code is fine.

## Per-unit grid

Legend: `R#` = has requirement-ID leak (M1) · all units need M2/L3 sweep · `rt` = reading_time fix (L2).

| Unit | M1 (R#) | M2 (codes) | L1 (density) | L2 (rt) | L3 (CV) | done |
|------|---------|-----------|--------------|---------|---------|------|
| U1 onboarding        | yes (R14) | yes | yes (Concept) | ok (8/~6) | yes | ⬜ PILOT |
| U2 explore           | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U3 operate-safely    | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U4 memory-context    | yes (R14×2) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U5 ship-feature      | — | yes | ok (good prose) | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U6 tdd               | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U7 debug             | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U8 git-pr            | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U9 legacy-refactor   | yes (R7.AC3) | yes | review | ok (11/~12) | yes | ⬜ |
| U10 spec-driven      | yes (keep teaching R1/R2.AC3; strip R3.AC2/R7.AC3) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U11 review           | yes (R7.AC3) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U12 commands-skills  | yes (R14, R7.AC3×2) | yes | review | **rt 8→10** | yes | ⬜ |
| U13 subagents        | yes (R7.AC3×2) | yes | review | **rt 8→12** | yes | ⬜ |
| U14 hooks            | yes (R14, R13×2) | yes | review | **rt 8→11** | yes | ⬜ |
| U15 mcp-vetting      | — | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |
| U16 automate-scale   | yes (R14, R12, R15) | yes | review | ok | yes | ⬜ |

**Cross-cutting files to sweep after units:** `course/capstone/{README,briefs,rubric,case-study}.md`,
`course/stuck.md`, `course/maintainer-guide.md` (maintainer-facing — `R#`/codes may be *appropriate*
here; sweep with judgment, don't strip load-bearing rubric `[Cn]` tags).

**Verification after each batch:** `make check` (links/refs/traceability must stay green — none of
these edits touch front-matter `requirements:`/`can_do:` or the rubric `[Cn]` tags, so traceability
is unaffected by design).

## Decisions (locked)

- **Cross-ref style: title-only** — "the **Operate safely** unit" / "**Explore a codebase**". No
  bare codes, no unit numbers in prose. (User, 2026-05-31.)

message.content: in both vscode and gitea the tags aren't populating. I'm seeing the raw tag text. This is pasted from gitea. Your baseline configuration (established here, taught later). This unit drops a known-good starter CLAUDE.md and a minimal .claude/settings.json into the practice codebase so every later lab starts from the same place. We're only establishing the baseline now.
